No Pills, No Needles: Killer Drugs So New Many Aren’t Even Illegal Yet
Lab-made drugs soaked into the pages of letters, books and even legal documents are being smuggled behind bars, killing inmates and frustrating investigators.
Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
Meridith Kohut for The New York Times

For most of human history, illicit drugs have been grown on the land. But that is changing, rapidly. Today, drugs are synthesized in illegal labs faster than law enforcement can keep track. A jail in Chicago became an unwitting frontier of this new war on drugs.

At first, inmates were having seizures and growing terribly ill. Then they began dying. Cook County jail found itself on the forefront of an explosion of new lethal synthetic drugs soaking the pages of books, letters and other seemingly-harmless paper.

Cook County jail is a walled surveillance state. But when inmates started dying of drug overdoses, officials realized that something as ordinary and ubiquitous as paper was being laced with drugs that the authorities could not see, much less stop.
Deadly new drugs are being churned out by fringe chemists in illicit labs faster than the authorities can identify them. More than 1,400 new synthetic drugs have been discovered since 2013 alone.

Fentanyl is the most notorious, but it’s far from the most powerful or the most addictive of these deadly synthetic drugs. Nitazenes, a class of synthetic opioid, can be 20 times more potent than fentanyl.

“Today is the most dangerous time in the history of the world to be using drugs,” said Dr. Andrew Monte, the head of the Rocky Mountain Poison Center. “That’s until tomorrow, when there’s a new drug.”
